takeaways: Hospital system Hoag paid $207M — $30M over the terminated auction bid — for the GSA's 90-acre Laguna Niguel Ziggurat site, a striking marker of how aggressively healthcare and senior-care operators are competing with developers for prime SoCal land.

Institutional conviction on office is returning: Brookfield is negotiating a 10% stake in Trinity/Norges' 6.2M sf Hudson Square portfolio at a $3.5B valuation, and Green Street's CPPI is up 4.1% year over year — even as the S2 Capital multifamily bust keeps Sun Belt distress in the headlines.

Senior housing supply hit a record low — under 1,500 new units in a quarter, just 0.7% growth — while occupancy marches toward 90%; Frontier's CEO says the winners will build now rather than wait for rate relief.

items.headline: Hoag pays $207M for GSA's 90-acre Ziggurat site, outbidding developers
items.summary: Newport Beach hospital system Hoag tied up the GSA's 90-acre Laguna Niguel Ziggurat campus for $207M ($2.3M/acre) — $30M more than the $177M Hilco Development/Jeff Pintar auction bid the GSA terminated after Hoag sued alleging collusion. CBRE ran the re-trade as-is with no due diligence period after a 157-bid auction fiasco. A healthcare system outbidding developers for a ~1M sf expansion site shows how fiercely health and senior-care operators are competing for SoCal land. (via The Promote)

items.headline: Brookfield eyes 10% of Hudson Square portfolio at $3.5B valuation
items.summary: Brookfield is negotiating to buy a 10% stake in Trinity Church and Norges' 6.2M sf, 13-building Hudson Square portfolio in Manhattan at a $3.5B valuation (~$560/sf), taking over as operating partner and likely displacing Hines. The area is a TAMI/tech magnet with Google, Disney, PayPal and Anthropic in the mix. It's a major institutional bet that Manhattan office values have found a floor. (via The Promote)

items.headline: S2 Capital's multifamily empire unravels in Sun Belt syndicator bust
items.summary: The Real Deal published a timeline of Dallas-based S2 Capital's financial unraveling — a marquee multifamily syndicator bust with Pimco playing workout puppet master, even as founder Scott Everett posts on LinkedIn as if nothing is wrong. Continued fallout from overleveraged Sun Belt multifamily is a signal for senior housing and multifamily operators watching for distressed acquisition opportunities. (via The Promote)

items.headline: Senior housing supply hits record low as occupancy nears 90%
items.summary: Fewer than 1,500 new senior housing units were added to primary markets in a recent quarter — just 0.7% year-over-year growth, the lowest since NIC MAP began tracking in 2006 — while industry occupancy climbs roughly 50 bps per quarter toward 90% and rent growth holds above 4%. The widening demand-supply gap underpins pricing power for operators like Lloyd Jones with existing communities. Cap rates are compressing as capital chases scarce stabilized assets. (via web search)

items.headline: Green Street: CRE prices up 4.1% year over year, exiting the downturn
items.summary: Green Street's Commercial Property Price Index held flat in June but is up 4.1% over the past 12 months, with values still 14% below the 2022 cycle peak. The sector-based recovery suggests pricing has bottomed for favored asset classes even as cap rates stay sticky on elevated rates. For senior housing owners, it supports a firming valuation backdrop heading into H2 2026. (via web search)

items.headline: Frontier CEO: now is the time to build senior living, not wait
items.summary: Frontier Senior Living is targeting 5 to 10 new developments over the next 12–16 months, emphasizing full-continuum properties with prominent independent living to attract baby boomers. CEO Greg Roderick argues rates, land and construction costs 'are what they are' and developers who wait will lose the window. A contrarian build-now stance amid the thinnest supply pipeline on record. (via web search)

items.headline: JSB Capital grabs Brandywine's Tysons towers for about $100M
items.summary: Jay Lobell's JSB Capital, with Cliff Cummings' Cummings Capital, is in contract to buy Brandywine's 1676 International Drive and 8260 Greensboro Drive in Tysons for roughly $100M ($215/sf), 85% leased with a 5.4-year WALT, closing in August. It's part of the struggling office REIT's plan to shed at least $300M in assets and exit Greater DC entirely; Hines separately bought its Austin tower for $151M. REIT retrenchment keeps feeding well-priced deals to private buyers. (via The Promote)

items.headline: Road to Housing Act becomes law without Trump's signature
items.summary: The bipartisan Road to Housing Act was enacted without President Trump's signature — a rare federal affordable-housing bill crossing the finish line. New federal incentives and financing mechanisms could improve the math on attainable senior housing and multifamily development. Worth watching how HUD implements the programs. (via The Promote)

items.headline: 2026 senior living outlook: demand leads as capital reopens
items.summary: Multi-Housing News' mid-year read on senior living finds demand-led fundamentals with capital markets reopening: cap rates compressing, property pricing rising faster than rental income, and more debt financing available for stabilized assets. New investors continue entering the space chasing strong operators. The setup favors sellers and well-run operating platforms. (via web search)

items.headline: CRE investors enter H2 2026 cautious as financing costs stay elevated
items.summary: Investors are entering the second half of 2026 balancing long-term optimism against persistent capital markets friction, with many reluctant to deploy significant capital until financing conditions improve. Higher Treasury yields keep debt costs elevated and complicate acquisition underwriting, pushing buyers beyond traditional financing sources. Discipline and creative capital stacks remain the playbook. (via web search)

items.headline: PropTech market hits $44.6B as AI moves from pilots to production
items.summary: The proptech market reached $44.59B in 2026, up from $40.19B in 2025, as AI shifts from experimentation to practical implementation across real estate operations. Organizations are adopting tools that streamline operations, cut costs and sharpen decision-making. For multi-site senior housing operators, the maturing vendor landscape lowers the risk of embedding AI in core workflows. (via web search)

items.headline: AI-driven proptech funding wave: Rebar $14M, Ownwell $50M
items.summary: AI is fueling a new wave of proptech investment, with deals of $14M–$50M closing across construction, commercial real estate and residential services — including Rebar's $14M raise for AI-generated commercial HVAC quoting and Ownwell's $50M to scale AI-powered property tax appeals. Investors are backing tools that attack specific operating-expense line items. Property tax and procurement automation are directly relevant to operators' NOI. (via web search)

items.headline: Leadership development tops HR priorities as innovation pressure rises
items.summary: Leadership development remains HR's top priority heading into 2026, with enabling innovation rising fast alongside it as organizations navigate technology adoption and economic uncertainty. Modern leaders are expected to pair strategic thinking with adaptability amid rapid market shifts. For operators scaling multi-site teams, bench strength is the constraint to watch. (via web search)

items.headline: HBR: six techniques to make feedback land with different employees
items.summary: New Harvard Business Review guidance notes different employees respond differently to the same feedback, and offers six techniques to ensure the message lands — from calibrating directness to timing the conversation. Practical reading for community-level managers running large frontline teams. (via web search)

items.headline: Neurowellness named top 2026 wellness trend by Global Wellness Summit
items.summary: The Global Wellness Summit's 2026 trends put neurowellness — using technology to regulate the nervous system — at the top, alongside 'emotional fitness' and a shift away from over-optimization toward simple defaults like light, air and movement. Resident programming built on these themes is a differentiator for engagement-focused senior living communities. (via web search)

items.headline: Connection-driven wellness displaces alcohol-centered socializing
items.summary: 2026 lifestyle trends show a marked shift from alcohol-centered socializing to connection-driven wellness — run clubs, movement 'snacks' through the day, and community-based fitness — while the gut-health market heads past $90B by 2030. The social-connection thread maps directly onto what senior living residents and their adult children increasingly shop for. (via web search)
